threats.title: SGX Instance Address Re-Registration via Missing addressRegistered Update in _replaceInstance
threats.hypothesis_description: When SgxVerifier.verifyProof replaces an SGX instance via _replaceInstance, the newInstance address is NOT added to the addressRegistered mapping. This allows the same address to be re-registered via registerInstance(), creating a duplicate valid instance ID. An attacker with access to a valid SGX attestation can obtain multiple instance IDs for the same enclave address, bypassing the one-instance-per-address invariant and circumventing the INSTANCE_EXPIRY mechanism by rotating between IDs.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code bug where _addInstances checks addressRegistered but _replaceInstance never sets it for newInstance. Any SGX enclave that has been used for proof verification and rotated keys can re-register the new address, gaining a second valid instance ID.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, ...) but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true; _addInstances at line 195 checks addressRegistered but won't find newInstance

preconditions: SGX instance has been used for at least one proof verification (triggering _replaceInstance)
automata_dcap_attestation is registered and functional in AddressManager
Attacker has a valid attestation for the newInstance address from the replaced instance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _replaceInstance at line 217 writes instances[id] but does NOT write addressRegistered[newInstance] = true
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - the gap exists after every verifyProof call

operational_questions: Can a replaced SGX instance reuse its attestation to call registerInstance for a second ID?
How does having multiple instance IDs affect the expiry bypass?
Is the attestation data reusable across multiple registerInstance calls?
validation_requirements: Trace _replaceInstance to confirm addressRegistered is never updated for newInstance
Test registerInstance with an address that was set via _replaceInstance
Verify that multiple instance IDs with the same address enable expiry circumvention

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: SGX enclave proves a block, replacing oldInstance with newInstance via verifyProof
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls registerInstance with valid attestation for newInstance address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Since addressRegistered[newInstance] is false, registration succeeds and creates a second instance ID
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker now has two valid instance IDs and can rotate between them to bypass expiry
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Access to valid SGX attestation for the newInstance address

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: SGX proof verification integrity compromised; expired keys can remain active
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Invalid proofs could be accepted if the SGX key has been compromised after its intended expiry
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner must manually deleteInstances for duplicate IDs

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single attacker with SGX access
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after first verifyProof call

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple instance IDs mapping to the same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: registerInstance calls for addresses already used in verifyProof
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Instance ID creation spike after proof verifications

threats.title: SGX Key Rotation Bypass via Self-Replacement with Same Address
threats.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not check if newInstance equals oldInstance. An SGX prover can set newInstance to be the same as oldInstance in their proof data, resetting the validSince timestamp to block.timestamp. This allows the same SGX key to remain valid indefinitely, bypassing the 180-day INSTANCE_EXPIRY and defeating the key rotation requirement designed to protect against side-channel attacks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _replaceInstance function unconditionally overwrites the instance with newInstance and current timestamp. There is no check that newInstance != oldInstance. The SGX enclave controls the newInstance address in the proof data and signs over it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, uint64(block.timestamp)); — no check that newInstance != oldInstance

preconditions: SGX instance is registered and valid
SGX enclave generates proof where newInstance == current instance address
Instance is within INSTANCE_EXPIRY period

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _replaceInstance unconditionally sets instances[id] with newInstance and fresh timestamp without comparing old vs new
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - prover controls newInstance in proof data

operational_questions: Does the SGX enclave software enforce key rotation, or is it left to the contract?
What side-channel risks materialize when the same key is used beyond 180 days?
Can this be detected by monitoring instance replacement events?
validation_requirements: Confirm no validation that newInstance != instances[id].addr in _replaceInstance or verifyProof
Test proof submission where newInstance == oldInstance to verify timestamp reset
Assess whether the ECDSA signature covers the newInstance to confirm enclave controls it

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: SGX enclave generates proof with newInstance set to its own current address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: verifyProof accepts the proof, calls _replaceInstance(id, addr, addr)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: instances[id].validSince is reset to current timestamp, extending validity by another 180 days
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat before each expiry to maintain the same key indefinitely
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Access to the SGX enclave signing capability

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Side-channel protection of key rotation is entirely defeated
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Compromised keys may remain active indefinitely
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires owner to deleteInstances or update contract code

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single prover with SGX enclave
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of SGX proof data format

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: InstanceAdded events where replaced address equals new address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Same instance address persisting beyond INSTANCE_EXPIRY
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Proof submissions with identical old/new addresses

threats.title: Guardian Proof Data Manipulation via Threshold-Triggering Approval
threats.hypothesis_description: In GuardianProver.approve(), the approval hash is computed as keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)) — the _proof parameter is NOT included in the hash. Guardians approve a (meta, transition) pair, but the actual proof.data passed to TaikoL1.proveBlock is determined entirely by the guardian who triggers the threshold. A malicious threshold-triggering guardian can inject arbitrary proof.data, specifically the RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND magic value, to return the liveness bond to the assigned prover — an action other guardians did not approve.
threats.investigation_rationale: The approval hash excludes _proof from its computation. When the threshold is met, only the calling guardian's _proof is forwarded to TaikoL1.proveBlock. Since proof.data controls RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND behavior, one guardian can alter economic outcomes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bytes32 hash = keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)); — _proof is not included in the hash; line 45: ITaikoL1(...).proveBlock(_meta.id, abi.encode(_meta, _tran, _proof)) uses the calling guardian's _proof

preconditions: At least minGuardians - 1 guardians have already approved the same (meta, tran) pair
The malicious guardian is the one whose approval triggers the threshold
The block has a non-zero livenessBond

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Approval hash excludes proof data but proof data controls bond economics
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires being a registered guardian (trusted role)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any guardian proof approval where the calling guardian triggers threshold

operational_questions: Can the order of guardian approvals be manipulated to control who triggers threshold?
What is the economic impact of unauthorized RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND on protocol solvency?
Should _proof be included in the approval hash?
validation_requirements: Confirm _proof is excluded from approval hash computation
Test scenario where threshold-triggering guardian passes RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND proof data
Verify that other guardians' intended proof.data is ignored

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for minGuardians - 1 guardians to approve a legitimate (meta, tran) pair
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Malicious guardian calls approve with same (meta, tran) but proof.data = RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND magic
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Threshold is met; GuardianProver calls TaikoL1.proveBlock with the malicious proof
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: LibProving checks RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND condition: proof.data.length == 32 && bytes32(proof.data) == RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND — transfers livenessBond to assignedProver
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single malicious guardian who can observe and time their approval

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Guardian multisig agreement is partially bypassed for proof.data
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Liveness bond returned without legitimate consensus; economic incentives distorted
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No automatic detection; requires monitoring proof.data in guardian proofs

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single guardian timing their approval
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of guardian approval flow and RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: TransitionProved events from guardian tier with non-zero livenessBond transfers
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND triggered for blocks where most guardians intended standard proof
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Specific guardian consistently being the threshold trigger

threats.title: Proposer Selective Tier Avoidance via Predictable Block Randomness
threats.hypothesis_description: The minimum proof tier for each block is determined by meta_.difficulty = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(block.prevrandao, b.numBlocks, block.number)), which is fully deterministic given known L1 state. A proposer can compute the required tier off-chain before submitting. On Mainnet, if the block would require TIER_SGX_ZKVM (0.1% probability, much more expensive), the proposer can simply wait for the next L1 block where the tier reverts to TIER_SGX, effectively bypassing the random high-security audit mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: All inputs to the difficulty hash are publicly observable before the proposer commits. The proposer has zero cost for checking and skipping unfavorable blocks. This undermines the statistical security guarantee of multi-tier proving.
preconditions: Proposer can observe current block.prevrandao, numBlocks, and block.number
Proposer has the ability to delay proposal by one or more L1 blocks
MainnetTierProvider is active (0.1% SGX_ZKVM requirement)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Randomness is deterministic from public on-chain values; proposer controls submission timing
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any proposer can exploit this

operational_questions: What is the real-world security impact if ZKVM tier is never triggered?
Can multiple proposers coordinate to ensure ZKVM blocks are never proposed?
Is there monitoring for blocks that consistently avoid ZKVM tier?
validation_requirements: Compute tier requirements for consecutive L1 blocks to verify predictability
Measure actual ZKVM tier occurrence rate in production
Confirm that all randomness inputs are observable before proposal submission

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compute keccak256(prevrandao, numBlocks, block.number) for current and next L1 blocks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If current block requires TIER_SGX_ZKVM (difficulty % 1000 == 0), wait for next L1 block
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit proposal only when the tier requirement is TIER_SGX (cheaper proving cost)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Over time, TIER_SGX_ZKVM is never triggered, reducing proof diversity

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No admin compromise, but proving tier diversity is undermined
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: ZKVM tier never activated; reduced security assurance
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol change to use commit-reveal randomness

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Statistical analysis of actual vs expected ZKVM tier occurrence
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: ZKVM tier never appearing despite expected 0.1% rate
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Proposers consistently proposing in specific L1 block positions

threats.title: Assigned Prover Permanently Locked Out From Higher-Tier Re-proving After Contest
threats.hypothesis_description: In _checkProverPermission, once a transition exits the initial state (tid==1, tier==0, inProvingWindow), the assigned prover is permanently banned from submitting any further proofs for that block (reverts with L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED). If the assigned prover's correct proof is contested, they cannot re-prove at a higher tier. A third-party must resolve the contest, becoming ts.prover. During verification, since ts.prover != assignedProver, the assigned prover loses half their livenessBond despite having proven correctly.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level design issue where the assigned prover is structurally penalized even when their proof was correct. The economic loss (livenessBond/2) creates a griefing vector: an attacker contests correct proofs to force the assigned prover into a loss, at a cost of only 3/4 of the contestBond.

proof_of_signal.pattern: else { if (isAssignedPover) revert L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED(); } — assigned prover is always rejected outside the initial proving window for tid==1, tier==0

preconditions: Assigned prover has submitted a correct first proof at some non-guardian tier
An attacker has sufficient TKO to pay the contestBond
A third-party higher-tier prover is available to resolve the contest

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assigned prover banned after initial proof; cannot participate in contest resolution for their own correct proof
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any block where the assigned prover has proven the first transition

operational_questions: What is the net economic impact on assigned provers from repeated contest griefing?
How does this affect the willingness of provers to participate as assigned provers?
Could this lead to a prover shortage and protocol stalling?
validation_requirements: Verify assigned prover cannot call proveBlock at higher tier after their initial proof
Calculate exact economic loss for assigned prover vs attacker cost
Test the full contest-resolution-verification flow

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Assigned prover submits correct SGX proof for block N
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker contests with different transition at SGX tier (pays 500 TKO contestBond; no SGX verification needed for contesting)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Third party (or attacker with different address) resolves at higher tier confirming original was correct
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Third party becomes ts.prover; during verification assigned prover loses livenessBond/2 (125 TKO)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Attacker needs TKO for contestBond (500 TKO, loses 375 net)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No admin compromise; economic incentive distortion
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Provers may refuse assigned prover role due to griefing risk
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol upgrade to fix permission logic

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: 500 TKO per contest (375 net loss per griefing)
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After assigned prover submits first proof, before higher-tier resolution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: TransitionContested events followed by same-transition higher-tier proofs
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Frequent contests of correct proofs from specific addresses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Assigned provers consistently losing livenessBond/2 despite correct proofs

threats.title: Guardian Emergency Proofs Blocked During Proving Pause Creates Protocol Deadlock
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoL1.proveBlock is gated by both whenNotPaused and whenProvingNotPaused modifiers. When proving is paused, guardians cannot submit emergency corrective proofs because GuardianProver.approve calls ITaikoL1.proveBlock, which will revert. This creates a dead-end state: if proving is paused after an invalid proof is submitted, the protocol cannot receive the guardian's corrective proof until proving is unpaused, leaving the invalid proof in place during the pause period.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guardians are the highest-authority provers designed as a safety net, yet they are blocked by the same pause mechanism as regular provers. This contradicts the intended trust model where guardians should be able to override any state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function proveBlock(...) external nonReentrant whenNotPaused whenProvingNotPaused — no exception for guardian tier proofs

preconditions: provingPaused is set to true by owner or chain_pauser
An invalid proof exists that needs correction by guardians
Owner/chain_pauser is unable or unwilling to unpause immediately

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: proveBlock applies whenProvingNotPaused uniformly; no bypass for TIER_GUARDIAN
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires chain_pauser or owner to have paused proving

operational_questions: Under what operational scenarios would proving be paused while guardian intervention is needed?
What is the maximum realistic pause duration before the owner can unpause?
Does this create an exploitable window for bridge message manipulation?
validation_requirements: Confirm GuardianProver.approve() reverts when provingPaused is true
Test the scenario of pausing proving with an active invalid proof
Verify that there is no alternative path for guardian proofs during pause

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Invalid proof is submitted for a block
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: chain_pauser pauses proving to prevent further damage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Guardians attempt to submit corrective proof but proveBlock reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol is stuck until proving is unpaused; verification halts entirely
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires proving to be paused (operational event)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Guardian safety net is neutralized during critical incidents
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete verification halt during proving pause
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires unpausing proving first, which may expose the protocol to the original threat
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All block verification, cross-chain state syncing

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - occurs naturally during operational incidents

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ProvingPaused events coinciding with unresolved contested transitions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed GuardianProver.approve transactions during pause periods
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Proving pauses during active dispute resolution

threats.title: GuardianProver Ownership Not Transferred to Timelock in Deployment Script
threats.hypothesis_description: In the DeployOnL1 script, GuardianProver is initialized with owner=address(0) (defaults to msg.sender), then setGuardians is called, then transferOwnership(timelock) is called. However, since EssentialContract inherits Ownable2StepUpgradeable, transferOwnership only sets pendingOwner — the actual owner remains msg.sender until acceptOwnership is called. The script calls acceptOwnership for sharedAddressManager and rollupAddressManager but NOT for GuardianProver. After deployment, the original deployer retains ownership of GuardianProver and can change guardian sets, upgrade the contract, or pause it.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script uses Ownable2Step pattern but does not complete the 2-step transfer for GuardianProver. This leaves the deployer with full control over the highest-authority proving mechanism.
deployment_assumptions: Deployment script is executed as-is without manual post-deployment ownership acceptance
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol: line 'GuardianProver(guardianProver).transferOwnership(timelock)' — no subsequent acceptOwnership call for guardianProver, unlike lines for sharedAddressManager and rollupAddressManager

proof_of_signal.pattern: GuardianProver(guardianProver).setGuardians(guardians, minGuardians); GuardianProver(guardianProver).transferOwnership(timelock); — no acceptOwnership call follows for guardianProver

preconditions: Deployment executed using DeployOnL1 script without manual post-deploy steps
No separate administrative process completes the 2-step ownership transfer
Deployer key remains accessible

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: acceptOwnership is called for AddressManagers but not for GuardianProver in the same script
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Deployer retains unintended owner privileges over guardian set
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent until ownership transfer is completed

operational_questions: Was acceptOwnership called for GuardianProver in a separate post-deployment transaction?
Who currently owns the GuardianProver contract on deployed networks?
Can the deployer key be used to modify the guardian set?
validation_requirements: Check on-chain owner() of GuardianProver on deployed networks
Verify that transferOwnership was called but acceptOwnership was not
Test that deployer can still call setGuardians after script execution

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify the deployer address from deployment transaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If deployer key is compromised, call setGuardians to replace guardian set with attacker-controlled addresses
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use compromised guardian set to approve arbitrary block transitions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Submit invalid state transitions via guardian proof to corrupt L2 state

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full control over guardian set — the highest-authority proving mechanism
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Attacker can approve arbitrary state transitions as guardian tier
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires the timelock (pendingOwner) to acceptOwnership, which it cannot do without being owner of itself in this context
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All guardian-tier proving, potentially all L2 state integrity

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time while deployer remains owner

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Check owner() return value on deployed GuardianProver

threats.title: SgxVerifier _replaceInstance Accepts Zero Address as New Instance
threats.hypothesis_description: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not validate that newInstance is not address(0). While _addInstances explicitly checks for zero address, _replaceInstance unconditionally writes the new instance. If an SGX enclave's proof data contains newInstance = address(0), the instance slot is effectively corrupted — instances[id].addr becomes address(0), and _isInstanceValid will reject it permanently. This instance ID becomes unusable without admin intervention.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetric validation between _addInstances (checks address(0)) and _replaceInstance (no check) is a code defect. While exploiting this requires a compromised or malfunctioning SGX enclave, it creates a permanent denial of service for the affected instance ID.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _replaceInstance writes instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, ...) without checking newInstance != address(0); compare _addInstances line 200: if (_instances[i] == address(0)) revert SGX_INVALID_INSTANCE()

preconditions: SGX enclave is compromised or malfunctioning
Proof data contains newInstance = address(0)
ECDSA signature is valid (signed by the current instance)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _addInstances validates address(0) but _replaceInstance does not — inconsistent input validation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None for triggering; admin needed for recovery (deleteInstances)

operational_questions: Can a compromised SGX enclave intentionally sign a proof with newInstance = address(0)?
How many instances could be corrupted before detection?
Is there automated monitoring for instance address corruption?
validation_requirements: Confirm _replaceInstance does not check for address(0)
Test verifyProof with newInstance = address(0) in proof data
Verify that corrupted instance ID cannot be recovered without deleteInstances

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised SGX enclave generates proof with newInstance = address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: verifyProof passes ECDSA verification (oldInstance signed the hash including address(0))
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Instance ID is permanently invalid; _isInstanceValid returns false for address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Access to compromised SGX enclave signing key

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Reduced SGX proving capacity until admin intervenes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner must call deleteInstances and re-register a new instance
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: SGX proof verification for affected instance ID

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any proof submission by compromised enclave

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: InstanceAdded events with instance address = address(0)
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Instance IDs suddenly failing validity checks
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Proof submissions immediately followed by instance invalidation

threats.title: Proving Window Indefinite Extension via Pause/Unpause Toggle by chain_pauser
threats.hypothesis_description: The proving window check in _checkProverPermission uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) as the window start time. Both pauseProving(false) and TaikoL1.unpause() reset lastUnpausedAt to current timestamp. A malicious chain_pauser can repeatedly toggle pauseProving(true)/pauseProving(false), each time resetting lastUnpausedAt. This keeps the assigned prover's exclusive window perpetually open (for tid==1, tier==0), preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions, stalling block verification indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lastUnpausedAt variable is reset on every unpause event. Combined with the proving window check that uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt), repeated toggling prevents the proving window from ever expiring.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bool inProvingWindow = uint256(_ts.timestamp).max(_state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) + _tier.provingWindow * 60 >= block.timestamp; — lastUnpausedAt is reset on each unpause, extending the window

preconditions: chain_pauser role is compromised or colluding with assigned prover
chain_pauser can call pauseProving(true) and then pauseProving(false) repeatedly
Assigned prover intentionally does not prove, keeping tid==1, tier==0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: pauseProving(false) resets lastUnpausedAt; _checkProverPermission uses lastUnpausedAt as window start; repeated toggles keep window open

operational_questions: Is chain_pauser separate from owner? Who controls it?
How often can pauseProving be toggled without detection?
What monitoring exists for proving pause events?
validation_requirements: Confirm lastUnpausedAt is reset on each pauseProving(false)
Test that repeated toggle keeps inProvingWindow true indefinitely
Verify that no other prover can override during the forced window

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Malicious chain_pauser and assigned prover collude
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Assigned prover never submits proof for new blocks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: chain_pauser toggles pauseProving every hour: true then false
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: lastUnpausedAt resets each time, keeping proving window open; other provers cannot submit first-transition proofs

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete protocol stall if all blocks' first transitions are blocked
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Verification halts; L2 chain progression stops
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires removing chain_pauser role via governance/timelock

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent ProvingPaused(true)/ProvingPaused(false) event pairs in short succession
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proving windows never expiring for first transitions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Pause toggle frequency exceeding operational norms

threats.title: TaikoTimelockController Admin Role Bypasses Minimum Delay for Instant Governance Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: TaikoTimelockController.getMinDelay() returns 0 for any caller with TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE, completely bypassing the 7-day minimum delay. The deployment script grants TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE to securityCouncil. If the securityCouncil key is compromised, an attacker can schedule AND execute operations in the same block, replacing critical contract addresses in AddressManager (bridge, signal_service, verifiers) with malicious contracts, draining bridged assets or corrupting L2 state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly returns 0 delay for admin role holders. Combined with EXECUTOR_ROLE granted to address(0) (open execution) and the security council having both PROPOSER_ROLE and TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE, any compromise of the securityCouncil key enables instant execution of arbitrary operations through the timelock.
deployment_assumptions: Security council key is compromised or has single point of failure
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol grants TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE to securityCouncil; TaikoTimelockController.sol line 'return hasRole(TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE, msg.sender) ? 0 : super.getMinDelay()'
preconditions: Attacker compromises the securityCouncil private key or multisig
securityCouncil has TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE (confirmed in deployment script)
securityCouncil has PROPOSER_ROLE (confirmed in deployment script)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: getMinDelay returns 0 for TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE holders, allowing instant schedule+execute

operational_questions: Is the securityCouncil a multisig? What is its threshold?
Are there any monitoring systems for timelock operations with 0 delay?
What contracts are owned by the timelock that could be targeted?
validation_requirements: Verify securityCouncil has TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE on mainnet deployment
Confirm getMinDelay() returns 0 when called by securityCouncil
List all contracts and operations controllable through the timelock

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Schedule operation to change AddressManager entries (e.g., signal_service, bridge, taiko_token) to attacker-controlled contracts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Drain bridged assets via malicious bridge/signal service implementation

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete protocol takeover — all dependency-injected contracts can be swapped
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total loss of bridged assets; L2 chain state corruption
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires hard fork or community-coordinated migration

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Instant execution once key is available

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Timelock operations scheduled and executed in same block
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Operations with 0 delay from TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE holder

threats.title: Residual TKO Token Accumulation in TaikoL1 Without Recovery Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: When proofs are overridden or contests are resolved in LibProving._overrideWithHigherProof, only a fraction (1/4 or 1/2) of the loser's bond is paid as reward. The remainder stays in the TaikoL1 contract permanently. Similarly, in LibVerifying.verifyBlocks, when ts.prover != assignedProver, livenessBond/2 is retained. Over time, these retained fractions accumulate as locked TKO in TaikoL1 with no mechanism to withdraw or redistribute them. While this doesn't directly enable theft, it permanently removes TKO from circulation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The bond resolution math consistently retains portions of bonds in the contract. There is no sweep, burn, or redistribution function for these retained tokens. This creates a permanent token sink that grows with protocol activity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reward = _ts.contestBond >> 2 (only 25% paid as reward); reward = _ts.validityBond >> 2 (only 25%); reward = _ts.validityBond >> 1 (only 50%); remaining stays in contract with no withdrawal path

preconditions: Normal protocol operation with contests and higher-tier overrides
No administrative function exists to recover accumulated tokens

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Bond resolution retains 50-75% of loser bonds in contract; no recovery function exists

operational_questions: What is the expected annual accumulation rate of locked TKO?
Does the accumulation affect TKO supply economics?
Should a governance-controlled sweep function be added?
validation_requirements: Track TKO balance of TaikoL1 over time vs active bond commitments
Calculate the excess (balance - sum of active bonds)
Verify no function exists to withdraw excess tokens

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an active attack — structural design issue
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Over time, TKO accumulates in TaikoL1 from bond slashing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tokens are permanently locked with no recovery mechanism
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Growing locked balance could affect TKO token economics

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: No compromise, but asset recovery impossible without upgrade
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Growing permanent token lock; potential supply impact over years
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add recovery function

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: TKO balance of TaikoL1 growing beyond sum of active bonds
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: No withdrawal transactions from TaikoL1 for excess tokens
